blurb: A low Rocky Mountain native forming spreading yellow-green mats.
toxic: Milky sap irritates skin/eyes — wear gloves; caution with kids/pets

care.hardiness: Hardy to at least USDA Zone 4 (possibly Zone 3), so entirely winter-hardy across Boulder and the Front Range (Zone 5b–6a). The top growth dies back fully in fall; the rhizomatous root system overwinters without any protection and re-sprouts reliably each spring.[1]
care.sun: Full sun is ideal — 6+ hours of direct light keeps growth compact and blooming freely. It will tolerate part shade but tends to get a bit leggier in lower light. At high altitude or on hot, south-facing slopes it shines; on the plains full sun is fine as long as drainage is good.[1]
care.soil: Well-drained to dry soils are essential. Horned spurge grows naturally in rocky, sandy, and clay-loam soils across the Intermountain West, and it handles the alkaline clay-loam typical of Front Range gardens without complaint. Avoid heavy, poorly drained spots — standing moisture is its main enemy. No amendment needed; lean soils actually suit it best.[1]
care.water: Xeric once established — this is a true dry-land native, adapted to the Front Range's 15–18 in. annual precipitation. Water new transplants through the first growing season to help them root in, then step back to occasional deep irrigation during prolonged summer drought. Overwatering or wet winter soils are the chief way to kill it.[1]
care.spacing: Plants spread slowly by rhizomes into a low mat, eventually reaching 1–2 ft wide and up to 2 ft tall. Space transplants or allow natural colonies to develop at 12–18 in. apart. The spreading habit makes it a useful groundcover on dry slopes, but it won't run aggressively in well-drained garden soils.[1]
care.bloom: Yellow-green cyathia (the characteristic spurge 'flowers') open in spring through early summer — roughly April to June on the Front Range, earlier on south-facing exposures. The blue-green foliage remains attractive through summer.[1]
care.feeding: None needed. This species thrives on lean, native-style soils; fertilizing encourages lush, floppy growth and is unnecessary.[1]
care.maintenance: Very low-maintenance. Cut back dead or winter-damaged stems in early spring before new growth appears. No deadheading required — the plant doesn't rebloom and the spent cyathia are inconspicuous. Divide or thin spreading clumps every few years if they outgrow their space; this is best done in early spring.[1]
care.selfsow: Spreads primarily by rhizomes rather than prolific seed. It slowly expands its mat but is not considered aggressive or weedy in garden settings — well-mannered compared to invasive spurges like leafy spurge (*Euphorbia esula*). Thin or divide as needed.[1]
care.troubles: Generally trouble-free in dry, well-drained conditions. The main risk is root rot in heavy, wet soils — site it carefully and don't overwater. All euphorbia sap is a skin and eye irritant: wear gloves and eye protection when pruning or dividing, and keep children and pets away from cut stems. The plant is often confused with the invasive leafy spurge; horned spurge is noticeably smaller and less aggressive.[1][2]
care.companions: Other dry-slope Front Range natives: blue grama grass, prairie smoke, Rocky Mountain penstemon, cushion phlox, and native sedums. Its yellow-green bloom color pairs well with blue/purple tones of native salvias and penstemons.[1]
care.planting: Plant container-grown nursery starts in spring (April to mid-May) or early fall (September) on the Front Range. Spring is the easiest window — soil is workable, temperatures are mild, and the plant has a full season to root in before winter. Fall planting works if done at least 6 weeks before hard frost so roots can anchor before the ground freezes. Site selection matters most: plant in a well-drained or even dry spot from the start, as moving an established mat later is difficult and disruptive to the rhizomes. In containers, horned spurge is not an ideal candidate: its spreading rhizomatous habit wants to run, and pot roots are vulnerable to both summer heat and winter freezing. If grown in a container, use a deep pot with excellent drainage, water sparingly, and provide insulation or bring indoors before temperatures drop well below freezing.[1]
care.propagation: Division in early spring is the primary and most practical method — dig sections of the rhizomatous mat just as new growth emerges, making sure each division has both roots and at least one shoot. Replant promptly and keep moist until established. Seed is the other viable route: sow outdoors in fall for natural winter cold stratification, or refrigerate seed in damp sand for 4–6 weeks before spring sowing. Like all euphorbias, the sap is a skin and eye irritant — wear gloves when dividing or cutting. Stem cuttings are possible in principle but rarely attempted; the rhizome-division method is far simpler and more reliable for this spreading native.[1][2]

edible.summary: Horned spurge is poisonous — like all Euphorbia its milky latex is a skin and eye irritant and the plant is toxic if eaten. No part is edible.[2][3]
edible.caution: Like all spurges, horned spurge bleeds a milky-white latex (the irritant compounds are diterpene esters) whenever a stem or leaf is broken. The sap is a skin and eye irritant — contact can cause mild to severe contact dermatitis, and eye contact can cause temporary to permanent corneal damage. Ingestion blisters the lips and mouth and can cause nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. Wear gloves (and eye protection when cutting) and keep the plant and its cut stems away from children and pets. Never put any part in the mouth.[2][3]
